title: Semantic Cache for LLM Requests
concept: A semantic cache stores past query-response pairs, checking if a new query has similar meaning (similarity search) to return cached answers instantly, saving API costs.

exercises.prompt: Why do we use a semantic cache for LLM requests?

options.text: To save API costs by reusing answers to similar questions.
To make the AI write creative fictional stories.
To delete all user query history daily.

exercises.explain: Semantic caches are designed to store past query-response pairs and check for similarity to save costs and speed up response times.

cards.title: Stop Paying Twice for AI!
cards.body: Every time you ask an AI a question, it costs money and takes a few seconds to think. But what if the AI already answered the exact same concept for someone else? What if we could reuse those answers even if the questions were phrased differently?

cards.title: What is Semantic Cache?
cards.body: A semantic cache is a smart memory system for AI. Instead of looking for exact words, it checks if a new question has a similar meaning to one it has answered before. If it does, it reuse the saved answer, saving time and money!

cards.title: The Smart Assistant
cards.body: Imagine you have a personal assistant who answers your questions. If you ask 'What is the capital of France?' and then a minute later ask 'Can you tell me Paris\'s country?', a normal assistant looks it up twice. A semantic assistant realizes they mean the same thing and hands you the answer instantly from their memory pad.

cards.title: Caching in Action
cards.body: Without a semantic cache, asking 'How do I bake bread?' and 'What is the recipe for bread?' triggers two expensive AI API calls. With a semantic cache, the system recognizes they ask the same thing, returns the cached answer for the second query, and saves money.

cards.title: Myth: It Must Be Word-for-Word
cards.body: Some believe caching only works if users type the exact same words. That is true for old databases, but semantic caches use vector similarity to understand that 'Hi there!' and 'Hello!' mean the same thing.

cards.title: Traditional vs. Semantic
cards.body: See how a semantic cache differs from a traditional database cache when handling different wordings.

left.text: Only matches 'How is the weather?' if the user types those exact characters.

right.text: Matches 'Is it raining?' with 'How is the weather?' by checking similarity.

exercises.prompt: When does a semantic cache return a saved answer?

options.text: Only when the user types the exact same characters.
When a new query has a very similar meaning to a past query.
Only when the AI server is completely offline.
When the user enters a password incorrectly.

exercises.explain: Semantic caches look for conceptual similarity, meaning 'Is it cold outside?' and 'What is the outdoor temperature?' will match because they ask for the same information.

exercises.prompt: True or False: Using a semantic cache can lower your AI API bills.
exercises.explain: Semantic caching drastically reduces LLM API costs because it intercepts similar queries and answers them instantly without querying the expensive LLM provider again.

exercises.prompt: Fill in the missing word to complete the description of semantic caching.
exercises.sentence: A semantic cache matches queries based on their meaning rather than exact words.
exercises.explain: Semantic caches focus on the meaning (semantics) of the query rather than the exact spelling or phrasing of the words.

exercises.prompt: Which system is acting as a Semantic Cache?

options.text: System A: Returns a match only when the user types 'metamorphosis'.
System B: Returns the saved answer for 'metamorphosis' when the user asks 'how caterpillar becomes butterfly'.
System C: Returns a random cooking recipe for any science question.

exercises.explain: System B is the AI-driven Semantic Cache because it recognizes that 'caterpillar to butterfly' and 'metamorphosis' refer to the same concept. System A is just doing keyword matching, and System C is randomly guessing.

exercises.prompt: You are building an AI chatbot for a busy customer support team. Why should you implement a semantic cache instead of a traditional exact-match database cache?

options.text: It rejects queries that don't match the database exactly.
It compares the meaning of the query to save costs on similar questions.
It translates all user questions into Spanish before saving them.
It makes the AI model run slower to double-check its accuracy.

exercises.explain: A semantic cache uses embeddings to find sentences with similar meaning (high semantic similarity) even if they use different words. A regular database cache looks for exact matches.
